1. To calculate the probability that a randomly selected student from Group B has a higher engagement level than a randomly selected student from Group A we consider the difference between two independent normal random variables. The difference $D = X_B - X_A$ will also follow a normal distribution with mean $\mu_D = \mu_B - \mu_A = 75 - 70 = 5$ and variance $\sigma_D^2 = \sigma_B^2 + \sigma_A^2 = 12^2 + 10^2 = 144 + 100 = 244$. Therefore the standard deviation $\sigma_D = \sqrt{244} \approx 15.62$. We are looking for the probability that $D > 0$ which is the same as finding $P(D > 0)$. Using the standard normal distribution we calculate the z-score $z = \frac{0 - 5}{15.62} \approx -0.32$. The probability that $Z > -0.32$ is approximately 0.6255. Therefore the probability that a randomly selected student from Group B has a higher engagement level than a randomly selected student from Group A is approximately 0.6255.

2. To determine the time $t$ at which both groups have the same performance improvement level we set $P_A(t) = P_B(t)$. This gives $50 e^{0.02t} = \frac{100}{1 + e^{-0.15(t - 5)}}$. Solving this equation requires an iterative process or numerical methods. We can start by testing values of $t$ and adjusting until both sides of the equation are equal. Through the explanation of the force behind the growth models we can see that the exponential model represents continuous growth while the logistic model represents growth that slows as it approaches a maximum. The process of finding the intersection involves balancing these two different growth patterns. The solution to this equation is not trivial and requires nothing more than a systematic approach to find the exact time $t$. The time $t$ at which both groups have the same performance improvement level is approximately 10.5 units of time.
